expanded_description: Learning to calculate volumes of three-dimensional solids using integration, including volumes by cross-sections and volumes of revolution using the disk, washer, and shell methods.

core_concept.explanation: Integration can be used to find volumes of three-dimensional solids by summing infinitesimal slices. For solids with known cross-sections, we integrate the cross-sectional area. For solids of revolution, we rotate a region around an axis and integrate using disks, washers, or cylindrical shells.

image.alt: Diagram showing different methods for finding volumes using integration
image.caption: Figure 299: Different methods for finding volumes—cross-sections, disks, washers, and shells.

sections.introduction: Just as we use integration to find areas under curves, we can extend this concept to find volumes of three-dimensional solids. The key idea is to slice the solid into thin pieces, find the volume of each slice, and sum them using integration. This approach works for solids with known cross-sections and solids formed by rotating regions around axes.

image.alt: Diagram showing how slicing a solid leads to volume calculation
image.caption: Figure 300: Slicing a solid into thin pieces allows us to calculate volume using integration.

key_concept.definition: The volume of a solid can be found by integrating the cross-sectional area: $V = \int_a^b A(x) dx$ where $A(x)$ is the area of the cross-section at position $x$.
key_concept.context: This formula works for any solid where we can express the cross-sectional area as a function of position.

principles.statement: A solid can be divided into thin slices perpendicular to an axis, and the volume is the sum of slice volumes.
principles.application: Each slice has volume $dV = A(x) dx$ where $A(x)$ is cross-sectional area.
principles.analogy: Like slicing a loaf of bread—the total volume is the sum of all slice volumes.

principles.statement: The cross-sectional area $A(x)$ depends on the shape of the slice and the function defining the solid.
principles.application: Common shapes include squares, rectangles, circles, triangles, and semicircles.
principles.analogy: Like different cookie cutters making different shaped cookies from the same dough.

principles.statement: Rotating a region around an axis creates a solid whose volume can be found using disk, washer, or shell methods.
principles.application: Choice of method depends on the axis of rotation and the region's shape.
principles.analogy: Like a potter's wheel—rotating clay creates a symmetric 3D object.

sections.introduction: When a solid has a known cross-sectional shape perpendicular to an axis, we can find its volume by integrating the cross-sectional area. The cross-sections can be squares, rectangles, triangles, circles, or any shape whose area we can express as a function of position.

process_flow.description: Determine the region in the xy-plane that forms the base of the solid.

image.alt: Diagram showing base region identification
image.caption: Step 1: Identify the base region in the xy-plane.

process_flow.description: Identify the shape of cross-sections perpendicular to the axis.

image.alt: Diagram showing cross-section shape
image.caption: Step 2: Determine the cross-section shape.

process_flow.description: Express the area $A(x)$ or $A(y)$ as a function of position.

image.alt: Diagram showing area function derivation
image.caption: Step 3: Find the cross-sectional area function $A(x)$.

process_flow.description: Write $V = \int_a^b A(x) dx$ with appropriate limits.

image.alt: Diagram showing volume integral setup
image.caption: Step 4: Set up the volume integral $V = \int_a^b A(x) dx$.

process_flow.description: Compute the definite integral to find the volume.

image.alt: Diagram showing integral evaluation
image.caption: Step 5: Evaluate the integral to find the volume.

worked_examples.title: Volume with Square Cross-Sections
worked_examples.problem: Find the volume of a solid whose base is the region bounded by $y = x^2$ and $y = 4$, with square cross-sections perpendicular to the x-axis.

step_by_step.explanation: The base is bounded by $y = x^2$ (parabola) and $y = 4$ (horizontal line).

step_by_step.step: Determine the side length of squares
step_by_step.explanation: At position $x$, the side length equals the vertical distance between curves.

image.alt: Diagram showing solid with square cross-sections
image.caption: Figure 301: Solid with square cross-sections perpendicular to x-axis.

worked_examples.title: Volume with Semicircular Cross-Sections
worked_examples.problem: Find the volume of a solid whose base is the region bounded by $y = \sqrt{x}$, $x = 4$, and $y = 0$, with semicircular cross-sections perpendicular to the x-axis.

step_by_step.explanation: The base is bounded by $y = \sqrt{x}$, $x = 4$, and the x-axis.
At position $x$, the diameter equals the vertical distance from x-axis to curve.
Area of semicircle is $A = \frac{1}{2}\pi r^2$ where $r = \frac{d}{2}$.

image.alt: Diagram showing solid with semicircular cross-sections
image.caption: Figure 302: Solid with semicircular cross-sections perpendicular to x-axis.

sections.introduction: When a region is rotated around the x-axis, it creates a solid of revolution. The disk method slices this solid into thin circular disks perpendicular to the axis of rotation. Each disk has volume $dV = \pi [f(x)]^2 dx$.

key_concept.definition: Disk Method (x-axis): When rotating $y = f(x)$ around the x-axis from $x = a$ to $x = b$, the volume is $V = \pi \int_a^b [f(x)]^2 dx$.
key_concept.context: This method works when the region touches the axis of rotation, creating solid disks with no holes.

worked_examples.title: Volume by Disks Around x-axis
worked_examples.problem: Find the volume of the solid formed by rotating the region bounded by $y = \sqrt{x}$, $x = 4$, and $y = 0$ around the x-axis.

step_by_step.explanation: Region bounded by $y = \sqrt{x}$, $x = 4$, and x-axis, rotated around x-axis.
At position $x$, the radius equals the distance from x-axis to curve.

step_by_step.step: Set up the disk method integral

worked_examples.concept_applied: Disk method for rotation around x-axis: $V = \pi \int_a^b [f(x)]^2 dx$.

image.alt: Diagram showing disk method for rotation around x-axis
image.caption: Figure 303: Disk method creates solid disks when rotating around x-axis.

worked_examples.title: Volume by Disks with Trigonometric Function
worked_examples.problem: Find the volume of the solid formed by rotating $y = \sin(x)$ from $x = 0$ to $x = \pi$ around the x-axis.

step_by_step.explanation: Region under $y = \sin(x)$ from $0$ to $\pi$, rotated around x-axis.

step_by_step.step: Set up the disk method integral

worked_examples.concept_applied: Disk method with trigonometric functions may require trig identities.

image.alt: Diagram showing solid from rotating sine curve
image.caption: Figure 304: Rotating $y = \sin(x)$ creates a symmetric solid.

sections.introduction: When a region is rotated around an axis but doesn't touch the axis, the resulting solid has a hole in the middle. The washer method accounts for this by subtracting the inner volume from the outer volume. Each washer has volume $dV = \pi([R_{outer}]^2 - [R_{inner}]^2) dx$.

key_concept.definition: Washer Method: When rotating a region between $y = f(x)$ (outer) and $y = g(x)$ (inner) around the x-axis, the volume is $V = \pi \int_a^b ([f(x)]^2 - [g(x)]^2) dx$.
key_concept.context: This method works when there's a gap between the region and the axis of rotation, creating washers (disks with holes).

worked_examples.title: Volume by Washers Around x-axis
worked_examples.problem: Find the volume of the solid formed by rotating the region bounded by $y = x^2$ and $y = x + 2$ around the x-axis.

step_by_step.explanation: Set $x^2 = x + 2$ to find bounds of integration.
On $[-1, 2]$, $y = x + 2$ is above $y = x^2$, so outer radius is $x + 2$.

step_by_step.step: Set up the washer method integral

worked_examples.concept_applied: Washer method subtracts inner volume from outer volume.

image.alt: Diagram showing washer method with outer and inner radii
image.caption: Figure 305: Washer method creates hollow solids with inner and outer radii.

worked_examples.title: Volume by Washers Around Horizontal Line
worked_examples.problem: Find the volume of the solid formed by rotating the region bounded by $y = x^2$ and $y = 4$ around the line $y = 4$.

step_by_step.explanation: Distance from $y = 4$ to curves. Outer radius is from $y = 4$ to $y = x^2$.

step_by_step.step: Set up the washer method integral
step_by_step.explanation: Since inner radius is 0, this becomes disk method.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Rotation around horizontal line requires measuring radius from that line.

image.alt: Diagram showing rotation around horizontal line y=4
image.caption: Figure 306: Rotation around $y = 4$ creates a solid with measured radii from that line.

sections.introduction: The shell method is an alternative to the disk/washer method. Instead of slicing perpendicular to the axis of rotation, we slice parallel to it, creating cylindrical shells. This method is often easier when rotating around the y-axis or when the disk method would require solving for x in terms of y.

key_concept.definition: Shell Method (y-axis): When rotating a region around the y-axis, the volume is $V = 2\pi \int_a^b x \cdot h(x) dx$ where $x$ is the radius and $h(x)$ is the height of each shell.
key_concept.context: The shell method is particularly useful when rotating around the y-axis or when the region is easier to describe in terms of x.

worked_examples.title: Volume by Shells Around y-axis
worked_examples.problem: Find the volume of the solid formed by rotating the region bounded by $y = x^2$, $y = 0$, and $x = 2$ around the y-axis.

step_by_step.explanation: Region under $y = x^2$ from $x = 0$ to $x = 2$, rotated around y-axis.
At position $x$, radius is $x$ and height is $y = x^2$.

step_by_step.step: Set up the shell method integral

step_by_step.explanation: Using disk method would require solving $x = \sqrt{y}$ and integrating with respect to y.
step_by_step.calculation: Shell method is simpler for this problem

image.alt: Diagram showing shell method for rotation around y-axis
image.caption: Figure 307: Shell method creates cylindrical shells when rotating around y-axis.

worked_examples.title: Volume by Shells with Two Curves
worked_examples.problem: Find the volume of the solid formed by rotating the region bounded by $y = x$ and $y = x^2$ around the y-axis.

step_by_step.explanation: At position $x$, radius is $x$ and height is the vertical distance between curves.
step_by_step.calculation: Radius $r = x$; Height $h = x - x^2$ (top minus bottom)

step_by_step.step: Set up the shell method integral

worked_examples.concept_applied: Shell height is the vertical distance between two curves.

image.alt: Diagram showing shell method with two curves
image.caption: Figure 308: Shell height equals the vertical distance between curves.

sections.introduction: Different volume problems are better suited to different methods. Understanding when to use each method saves time and reduces errors. The choice depends on the axis of rotation, the shape of the region, and which variable is easier to work with.

image.alt: Flowchart showing how to choose between disk, washer, and shell methods
image.caption: Figure 309: Method selection depends on axis of rotation and region shape.

classifications.value: Use when region touches the axis of rotation
classifications.behavior: Best for rotation around x-axis with region bounded by x-axis, or rotation around y-axis with region bounded by y-axis

examples.explanation: Region bounded by $y = f(x)$ and x-axis, rotated around x-axis

image.alt: Diagram showing when to use disk method
image.caption: Figure 310: Disk method when region touches axis of rotation.

classifications.value: Use when region doesn't touch the axis of rotation
classifications.behavior: Best for rotation when there's a gap between region and axis

examples.explanation: Region between two curves, rotated around an axis neither curve touches

image.alt: Diagram showing when to use washer method
image.caption: Figure 311: Washer method when there's a gap from axis.

classifications.value: Use when rotation is around y-axis or when disk method is difficult
classifications.behavior: Best when solving for x in terms of y would be difficult

examples.explanation: Region rotated around y-axis, especially when function is $y = f(x)$

image.alt: Diagram showing when to use shell method
image.caption: Figure 312: Shell method often easier for y-axis rotation.

worked_examples.title: Comparing Methods for Same Problem
worked_examples.problem: Find the volume of the solid formed by rotating the region bounded by $y = x^2$ and $y = 4$ around the y-axis. Solve using both shell and washer methods.

step_by_step.explanation: Shells parallel to y-axis. Radius = $x$, Height = $4 - x^2$.
Washers perpendicular to y-axis. Need $x$ in terms of $y$: $x = \sqrt{y}$. Outer radius = $\sqrt{y}$, Inner radius = 0.
Both methods give the same answer. Shell method was slightly easier (no need to solve for x).

worked_examples.concept_applied: Different methods can solve the same problem; choose the easier one.

image.alt: Diagram comparing shell and washer methods for same problem
image.caption: Figure 313: Both shell and washer methods give the same volume.

sections.section_title: 3.12.7 Common Pitfalls and How to Avoid Them
sections.introduction: Understanding common mistakes when calculating volumes helps prevent errors and builds stronger integration skills.

classifications.value: Radius must be measured from axis of rotation
classifications.behavior: Always measure radius as distance from axis of rotation to the curve

examples.explanation: Using $y$ as radius when rotating around $y = 2$ instead of $|y - 2|$.

image.alt: Warning about radius measurement
image.caption: Figure 314: Radius must be measured from the axis of rotation.

classifications.value: Disk for solids, Washer for hollow solids
characteristics: Using disk when there's a hole
classifications.behavior: Use washer method when there's a gap between region and axis of rotation

examples.explanation: Using disk method $\pi \int R^2 dx$ instead of washer method $\pi \int (R^2 - r^2) dx$.

image.alt: Warning about disk vs washer
image.caption: Figure 315: Use washer method when there's a hole in the solid.

classifications.behavior: Disk/washer perpendicular to axis uses dx for x-axis, dy for y-axis; shells parallel to axis use opposite

examples.explanation: Using dx for washer method when rotating around y-axis (should use dy).

image.alt: Warning about integration variable
image.caption: Figure 316: Integration variable must match slicing direction.

classifications.value: Height is vertical distance between curves
classifications.behavior: Shell height = (top curve) - (bottom curve), always positive

examples.explanation: Using $x^2 - x$ instead of $x - x^2$ for height (getting negative volume).

image.alt: Warning about shell height
image.caption: Figure 317: Shell height must be top curve minus bottom curve.

classifications.value: Limits must match the region bounds
classifications.behavior: Find intersection points of curves to determine correct limits

examples.explanation: Using $x = 0$ to $x = 4$ instead of finding actual intersection points.

image.alt: Warning about integration limits
image.caption: Figure 318: Find intersection points to determine correct limits.

classifications.value: Disk and washer formulas include π
classifications.behavior: Remember: $V_{disk} = \pi \int R^2 dx$ and $V_{washer} = \pi \int (R^2 - r^2) dx$

examples.explanation: Writing $\int R^2 dx$ instead of $\pi \int R^2 dx$.

image.alt: Warning about missing pi
image.caption: Figure 319: Don't forget π in disk and washer formulas.

sections.introduction: Apply your understanding of volume calculations to these comprehensive practice problems.

worked_examples.title: Comprehensive Cross-Section Problem
worked_examples.problem: Find the volume of a solid whose base is the region bounded by $y = 4 - x^2$ and $y = 0$, with equilateral triangle cross-sections perpendicular to the x-axis.

step_by_step.explanation: The base is bounded by $y = 4 - x^2$ (parabola) and $y = 0$ (x-axis).

step_by_step.step: Determine the side length of triangles
step_by_step.explanation: At position $x$, the side length equals the vertical distance from x-axis to curve.
Use symmetry to simplify (integrand is even).

image.alt: Diagram showing solid with triangular cross-sections
image.caption: Figure 320: Solid with equilateral triangle cross-sections.

worked_examples.title: Comprehensive Disk/Washer Problem
worked_examples.problem: Find the volume of the solid formed by rotating the region bounded by $y = x^2$ and $y = 2x$ around the line $y = 4$.

step_by_step.explanation: Distance from $y = 4$ to curves. On $[0, 2]$, $y = 2x$ is above $y = x^2$.

step_by_step.step: Set up the washer method integral

worked_examples.concept_applied: Washer method with rotation around horizontal line $y = 4$.

image.alt: Diagram showing washer method with rotation around y=4
image.caption: Figure 321: Washer method with rotation around $y = 4$.

worked_examples.title: Comprehensive Shell Method Problem
worked_examples.problem: Find the volume of the solid formed by rotating the region bounded by $y = \sqrt{x}$, $y = 0$, and $x = 9$ around the line $x = 9$.

step_by_step.explanation: Region under $y = \sqrt{x}$ from $x = 0$ to $x = 9$, rotated around $x = 9$.
At position $x$, radius is distance from $x$ to $x = 9$, height is $\sqrt{x}$.

step_by_step.step: Set up the shell method integral

worked_examples.concept_applied: Shell method with rotation around vertical line $x = 9$.

image.alt: Diagram showing shell method with rotation around x=9
image.caption: Figure 322: Shell method with rotation around $x = 9$.

worked_examples.title: Method Selection Problem
worked_examples.problem: For the region bounded by $y = x^3$, $y = 8$, and $x = 0$, determine which method (disk, washer, or shell) is easiest for finding the volume when rotating around (a) the x-axis, (b) the y-axis, (c) the line $y = 8$.

step_by_step.explanation: Region doesn't touch x-axis, so washer method. Would need $x = y^{1/3}$, integrating with respect to y.
step_by_step.calculation: Washer method with dy: $V = \pi \int_0^8 ((y^{1/3})^2 - 0^2) dy$ - moderate difficulty

step_by_step.explanation: Region touches y-axis, so disk method. Easy with respect to y.
step_by_step.calculation: Disk method with dy: $V = \pi \int_0^8 (y^{1/3})^2 dy = \pi \int_0^8 y^{2/3} dy$ - easiest

step_by_step.explanation: Region touches $y = 8$, so disk method. Can use dx or dy.
step_by_step.calculation: Disk method with dx: $V = \pi \int_0^2 (8 - x^3)^2 dx$ - moderate difficulty

step_by_step.explanation: Choose the easiest method for each case.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) Washer with dy; (b) Disk with dy (easiest); (c) Disk with dx or shell with dx

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Washer method (dy); (b) Disk method (dy) - easiest; (c) Disk or shell method
worked_examples.concept_applied: Method selection depends on axis of rotation and which variable is easier.

image.alt: Diagram showing region with different rotation axes
image.caption: Figure 323: Different rotation axes require different methods.

summary: Section 3.12 covered calculating volumes of solids using integration. We learned three main approaches: (1) Volumes by cross-sections where $V = \int_a^b A(x) dx$ and $A(x)$ is the cross-sectional area (squares, triangles, semicircles, etc.); (2) Disk method for solids of revolution where region touches the axis: $V = \pi \int_a^b [R(x)]^2 dx$; (3) Washer method for hollow solids where region doesn't touch the axis: $V = \pi \int_a^b ([R_{outer}]^2 - [R_{inner}]^2) dx$; (4) Shell method for rotation around y-axis or when disk method is difficult: $V = 2\pi \int_a^b (\text{radius})(\text{height}) dx$. The disk method creates solid disks, washer method creates hollow washers (disks with holes), and shell method creates cylindrical shells. Method selection depends on the axis of rotation and which variable is easier to work with. For x-axis rotation, use disk/washer with dx; for y-axis rotation, shells with dx or disk/washer with dy often work best. Common pitfalls include measuring radius from wrong reference (must be from axis of rotation), confusing disk and washer methods, using wrong integration variable, incorrect shell height (must be top minus bottom), wrong limits of integration (find intersection points), and forgetting π in disk/washer formulas. Volume by integration extends the concept of area under curves to three dimensions, making it one of the most powerful applications of definite integrals in calculus.
